skill.name: nano-image-generator
skill.description: Generate visual assets with Gemini image generation from text prompts and optional reference images for style transfer, character consistency, and layout exploration.
skill.summary: Create image assets from prompts with optional reference images and configurable size or aspect ratio.
skill.author: lxfater

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, crypto, and obfuscation patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, image magic bytes, and base64 image handling. Confirmed risks are legitimate but material: the script requires a Gemini API key, sends prompts and reference images to Google, and reads or writes local files selected by the user. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: API Key Stored Directly In Source Code
medium_findings.description: The setup instructions tell users to replace a placeholder inside the Python script with their real Gemini API key. This can expose credentials through commits, backups, shared workspaces, or logs, even though no malicious exfiltration was found.
medium_findings.recommendation: Use an environment variable or secret manager instead of editing the API key into source code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation and script explicitly instruct users to place a real API key in source. This is a confirmed credential-handling risk, but it does not show malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Prompts And Reference Images Sent To External API
medium_findings.description: The script sends the text prompt and base64-encoded reference images to the Gemini API endpoint. This is expected for the skill, but users may accidentally transmit sensitive images or proprietary visual material.
medium_findings.recommendation: Warn users that prompts and reference images are sent to Google, and avoid sensitive inputs unless policy allows it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The request construction clearly includes prompt text and inline image data before posting to the Google endpoint. The behavior is legitimate for image generation but has privacy impact.

medium_findings.title: Local File Read And Write Operations
medium_findings.description: The script reads caller-provided reference image paths and writes generated bytes to a caller-provided output path. This is normal functionality, but it should be treated as filesystem access with data disclosure risk when reference files are sensitive.
medium_findings.recommendation: Run the skill from a limited workspace and review reference image paths before sending them to the provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The read and write paths come from user arguments and are required for the tool. Risk depends on how users select files and output paths.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer classified many Markdown backticks and usage examples as Ruby or shell execution. These are documentation snippets and option tables, not executable code paths inside the skill runtime.
low_findings.recommendation: No action required for these documentation examples, but reviewers should distinguish examples from runtime command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced code blocks or inline Markdown examples. No dynamic command execution API is present in the Python script.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation Findings Are Image Encoding Logic
low_findings.description: Hex byte literals are used to detect image formats, and base64 is used to decode the API image response. This is normal image processing, not evasive obfuscation.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep the image-format checks readable and documented; no security remediation is required for this pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The byte strings match common image magic bytes, and base64 decoding is applied to the provider image response. The semantic context is image handling.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines do not implement cryptography or password hashing. They are skill descriptions, feature text, or CLI help text for image generation.
low_findings.recommendation: No cryptographic remediation is required based on the reviewed lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic function or weak algorithm use at these locations. The static signal appears to come from unrelated text patterns.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Included In Request URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The script appends the Gemini API key to the request URL query string. Query-string secrets can be captured by request logging or error reporting systems.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Prefer provider-supported authorization headers when available, and avoid printing full request URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is built with key={api_key} and then used in the request. This is a real exposure pattern, though common in some Google API examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reference Images Encoded For Network Transfer
dangerous_patterns.description: User-selected reference images are read from disk, base64 encoded, and embedded into the outbound API payload. This is expected behavior but can expose sensitive local images.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Require users to confirm reference images are approved for external processing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code reads image bytes and places base64 data into inlineData for the API payload. This is clear data transfer behavior with legitimate purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Controlled Output Path Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated image bytes are written to the output path supplied by the caller, and parent directories are created when needed. This can overwrite files if users choose an unsafe destination.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Use project-local output paths and avoid pointing output at existing important files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write path is caller-controlled and the behavior is visible in the script. The practical risk is limited because image extensions are normalized.

content.value_statement: Teams need quick visual assets without leaving their coding workflow. This skill generates images from prompts and reference images with controllable size and aspect ratio.
actual_capabilities: Generates images from text prompts through the Gemini image API.
Uses up to 14 reference images for style transfer or character consistency.
Supports square, portrait, landscape, and cinematic aspect ratios.
Detects returned image format and saves with a matching file extension.
Creates output directories when saving generated images.
limitations: Requires a user-provided Gemini API key before generation works.
Current setup asks users to place the API key directly in the script.
Prompts and reference images are sent to Google for processing.
Generated text inside images may be inaccurate or visually inconsistent.

use_cases.title: Create App Icons
use_cases.description: Generate square icon concepts for apps, tools, or internal prototypes.

use_cases.title: Produce Marketing Visuals
use_cases.description: Create banners and social visuals with specific aspect ratios and visual direction.

use_cases.title: Keep Character Style Consistent
use_cases.description: Use reference images to create new scenes while preserving character or style direction.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Image Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a single image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a friendly robot mascot waving, with a clean modern style and a square aspect ratio.

prompt_templates.title: App Icon Concept
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a product icon with clear visual constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a minimalist app icon for a task manager. Use a bold checkmark, calm colors, soft depth, and a 1:1 format.

prompt_templates.title: Reference Style Match
prompt_templates.scenario: Use one reference image for visual consistency.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a new website hero image that keeps the same color palette and illustration style as the provided reference image.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Asset Campaign Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a consistent visual set for a productivity launch: one hero banner, one social card, and one square icon concept.

output_examples.input: Generate a square app icon for a weather app with a bright sun and clean flat design.
output_examples.output: A local square image file is saved with the correct image extension for the returned format.

output_examples.input: Create a 16:9 website banner for a productivity tool using blue and white geometric shapes.
output_examples.output: A wide banner image is generated and saved to the requested project path.

output_examples.input: Use this character reference and place the same character in a warm cafe scene.
output_examples.output: A new scene is generated using the supplied reference image for character consistency.

best_practices: Use environment variables for API keys instead of editing secrets into source files.
Use only reference images that are approved for external API processing.
Specify purpose, style, composition, aspect ratio, and color direction in prompts.
anti_patterns: Do not commit a real Gemini API key into a repository.
Do not send private customer images as references without approval.
Do not rely on generated image text for exact labels or legal copy.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It generates image files from text prompts and optional reference images using Gemini image generation.

faq.question: Does it support reference images?
faq.answer: Yes. The script accepts repeated reference image arguments and limits them to 14 images.

faq.question: Which aspect ratios are supported?
faq.answer: It supports square, portrait, landscape, mobile, and cinematic ratios listed in the script options.

faq.question: Does it need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. A Gemini API key is required before the script can call the image API.

faq.question: Are local files sent outside the workspace?
faq.answer: Reference images are read locally and sent to Google as part of the generation request.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings about API key handling, external processing, and local file access.
